narrative: As a white male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model looked at your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10—well below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 28 compared to the usual 36.7, and your rankings in school deciles from early and later years, along with plans to attend full-time like most applicants. It predicted an 82.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected: law schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, also much lower than the average around 5.7, reinforced concerns about early struggles that often predict bar exam challenges. Your LSAT score further tipped the scales toward failure, as it stood out as notably below what the model sees in successful candidates. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 92%), and factors like family income played lesser roles, these top academic indicators dominated the decision, painting a picture riskier than for typical applicants who rank higher in deciles and score stronger on entrance exams. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal qualities, but rather how your metrics stack up against patterns in the data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted tutoring to improve your decile-level performance in future endeavors. Building a stronger academic foundation now could shift future predictions in your favor and open doors to law school. Many students in similar situations have rebounded by focusing on these areas—your next steps could make all the difference.
